description: A19FJ - Day 1, part 5: LM extraction and S-IVB separation

tokens: Gene Cernan indicates he's going to have the camera off for about 10 minutes while they do some work.
We'll continue to stay up in case we get any communications.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: If you're ready to copy, we have a P30 PAD evasive maneuver, SPS/G&N: 63556, plus 0.91, minus 0.21; 004:39:09.00; Noun 81 is plus 3 balls 51, plus 5 balls, plus 0019.0.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That's just the right speed; I got gloves on yet.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Just go the way you are.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The rest of the pad is NA, and no ullage.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We had a handover to Goldstone and lost you for a while.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I've got you on omni, and I'll copy the rest of the pad on omni and get High Gain back again.
I got roll of 061, pitch of 255, and give me Yaw.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Noun 81 - and plus 0005.1, plus all balls, plus 0019.0; roll 061, 255, 358; Delta-VTis 0019.7, burn time is 0:03, and Delta-VCis 0015.0.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The evasive PAD details are as follows:Purpose:The PAD is for a short burn that takes the Apollo stack away from the S-IVB.

It also acts as a short test of the SPS engine, important given its critical function in returning the crew to Earth.Systems:The burn would be made using the SPS engine under the control of the Guidance and Navigation system.CSM Weight (Noun 47):63,556 pounds (28,829 kg).Pitch and yaw trim (Noun 48):+0.91° and -0.21°.Time of ignition (Noun 33):4 hours, 39 minutes, 9.00 seconds.Change in velocity (Noun 81), fps (m/s):x, +5.1 (+1.6); y, 0; z, +19.0 (5.8).
These are with respect to the Local Vertical/Local Horizontal.Spacecraft attitude:Roll, 61°; Pitch, 255°; Yaw, 358°.

This attitude is with respect to the launch pad REFSMMAT.HA, expected apogee of resulting orbit (Noun 44):Not applicable.HP, expected perigee of resulting orbit (Noun 44):Not applicable.Delta-VT, the total velocity change resulting from the burn:19.7 fps (6 m/s).Burn duration or burn time:3 seconds.Delta-VC:15.0 fps (4.6 m/s).
This figure, when entered into the EMS velocity display, can be used as a backup method of shutting down the engine.No ullage will be required as the SM propellant tanks are full.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The S-IVB's getting ready to do an auxiliary hydraulic pump cycling which you may feel, and we will have a non propulsive vent in a few minutes, also.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I would reckon that possibly 10, latches 10, 3 - 3, and 4 are probably going to be one shot in progress from the position of the bungee, but they all are automatically made.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Young, from the1969 Technical debrief:"All the latches were engaged solidly.
I felt around the edge, over on the bungees and springs.
Springs 3, 4, and 10 were still partially compressed.
In other words, they weren't all the way up to the top of the bungee, and that indicated they'd be one shot to cock and they were."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And it looks just like - just like downtown up here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're just getting the hatch area squared away at this time.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We just - just now completed that.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Young, from the1969 Technical debrief:"I took my helmet and gloves off to attach the 1M umbilical.
The umbilical configured normally and we applied power, and that was normal.
We were suited from pre-TLI through latch verification.
We installed the umbilicals, vented the probe, and reinstalled the hatch."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're reading 2 volts on system test meter 4D, and it just bounced back to four-tenths of a volt, so I guess the heaters are cycling or something in the LM.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: TELCOM see's the LM current; it looks nominal.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: When we pressurized the LM, the Mylar all blew out of the tunnel hatch there, and we have got a spacecraft that has beaucoup insulation in it here.
It looks like it didn't leave a big enough hole.
Just like the same way when the LM forward tunnel insulation used to blow out.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Young, from the1969 Technical debrief:"When we opened the hatch and pulled it down into the spacecraft it was obvious that the Mylar, bonded insulation, had broken loose and we had fiberglass insulation all over the inside of the spacecraft.
We shoved the hatch in the bag and crawled back over in the tunnel to check the latches."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: From theApollo 10 Mission Report:"When the Lunar Module cabin was first pressurized, the thermal coating on the Command Module hatch came off in pieces.
The insulation blanket vent holes were plugged, producing the damage.

One possibility is that the preflight baking of the hatch at 900°F for 15 hours weakened the insulation to the extent that internal pieces of insulation broke loose and plugged the holes during tunnel depressurization.
Another possibility is that the vent holes were inadvertently sealed when the insulation blanket was potted with RTV or when the H-film tape was installed on the hatch surface."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You lost every bit of the Mylar in the back side of the hatch?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Not every bit, but a whole bunch of it.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Oh, just a little of it, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: But, of course, they do away with it on 107 and subs.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: John Young is making the point that the Mylar insulation will not be installed on CM-107 (Apollo 11) and all subsequent CMs.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You got lots of pieces floating around?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 3 hours, 54 minutes.
We have 2 to 3 minutes of tape at this conference at the Cape.
We'll play that for you now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We still show the EDS power, On, and the EDS breakers, Closed.
Would you turn the power off and open the breakers, if you've got a second?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We've got the logic On, and we're standing by for your Go for Pyro Arm.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: The pyrotechnic devices that will separate the LM from the S-IVB are about to be armed.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You have our Go for Pyro Arm, 10.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The S-IVB is still venting non-propulsively.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We're going to separate in just a minute.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We'd like you to stand by until that vent's over in about 2 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We're standing by for your Go for Sep.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The vent's over; we're Go for Sep.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Understand that we are Go for Sep.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Houston, I'll turn the TV on, on this one.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'm going to count down to Sep.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We have TV coming up again right now.
The Command Module Service Module and the LM have just separated from the S-IVB.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Snoopy's coming out of the doghouse.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Clip courtesy John Stoll, ACR Senior Technician at NASA Johnson.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, we have Cryo pressure light; O2tank 1 and 2 are reading about 800 [psia].
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Both O2cryogenic storage tanks are indicating that their pressure has dropped below the normal minimum of 865 psia.
Tank pressures and quantities are monitored on meters located on MDC 2 (Main Display Console 2).
The caution and warning system (Cryo Press) will alarm when oxygen pressure in either tank exceeds 950 psia or falls below 800 psia.
The hydrogen system alarms above 270 psia and below 220 psia.
Since a common lamp is provided, reference must be made to the individual pressure and quantity meters (MDC 2) to determine the malfunctioning tank.
Tank pressures, quantities, and reactant temperatures of each tank are telemetered to MSFN.
The manual mode of operation bypasses the pressure switches, and supplies power directly to the heaters and/or fans through the individual control switches.
It can be used in case of automatic control failure, heater failure, or fan failure.
Two parallel dc heaters in each tank supply the heat necessary to maintain design pressures.
Two parallel 3-phase ac circulating fans circulate the fluid over the heating elements to maintain a uniform density and decrease the probability of stratification.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 10, we'd like to have you turn the fans on.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Houston, we're maneuvering around right now, acquired the S-IVB, going into SEP attitude.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Houston, that was fans in Auto or Off on the O2?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We can see the S-IVB now.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: John's looking at it out the hatch window.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Houston, it's the S-IVB in the TV right now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We got it right over the quad - thrusters there, Gene.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I'll see if I can bring it into you.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It looks like the dome there of the S-IVB is gold.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: It looks like we got good separation, distance and no problems.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: It looks like about the size of a quarter to us here, Tom.
What do you estimate your range?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I'd say at least 300 feet, now.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We have the S-IVB; the Sun's real bright on it.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That LM antenna is showing up real bright.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Von Braun, Lee James, Kurt Debus, and Rocco Petrone thanks a lot for all the people who worked on the great ride.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We're going out to the networks now, I think.
They probably heard it; we'll pass it on, though.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: A few thousand people worked on that machine, and we sure appreciate it.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Did you move the camera, Gene?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: It's out the hatch window; John's got it now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: In fact, you can see the S-IVB's attitude, thrusters firing, now.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We haven't been able to pick that up yet; S-IVB's really good.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I'd estimate now that we're a good 300 to 400 feet away.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We're starting to see one side of it.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: "Eagle-Eyes" Cooper said he can see your thrusters firing up there on the S-IVB.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I can't even see them from here.
He must be about 2,500 miles away.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You're looking out the wrong window.
transcript.speaker: Cooper

tokens: I'm sure going to like Snoopy, because that's all I'm going to see.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Distance from the Earth now 13,531 miles, velocity 16,063 feet per second.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I've got the S-IVB out my window now.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I've got the S-IVB out of my window, here.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We'd like you to keep the camera moving around so we don't burn anything.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Can you see all the particles around us?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: All we got in the S-IVB, and it's a real bright blob.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Is your ALC [Automatic Light Compensation] switch on Inside or Exterior/Interior?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: On the black and white it is.
It took all that washout out.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie, we're looking for the Earth right now.
We'd like to show it too, but we can't Find it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We want to get ahead and get some other things done.
We're going to go ahead and turn off the TV for a while.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: This to Apollo Control at 4 hours, 10 minutes.
Tom Stafford indicates that's all of the television for a while.
We're sure he'll give us-enough notice when he wants to come back up with it.
We're 28 minutes away from the nominal evasive maneuver time, however, both the ground and the spacecraft are GO for this maneuver and at crew option they could perform it early.
This will be a 19.7 feet per second burn with the service propulsion system, the big engine in the service module, retrograde burn.
It will put the S-IVB out ahead of the Apollo 10 spacecraft, the dock configuration.
Then shortly after the burn the remaining propellants in the S-IVB will be vented through the engine, be a propulsive vent.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're still slowly moving away from the S-IVB, but that rascal is boresighted right at us.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: There will be enough energy in this propulsive vent to put the S-IVB on a trajectory that will go around the trailing edge of the moon and then into solar orbit.
This is the sling shot maneuver.
Distance from earth now 14,449 miles, Apollo 10's velocity now 15,635 feet per second.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 4 hours, 14 minutes.
We still have about 3 minutes worth of tape left that accumulated during the news conference at the Kennedy Space Center.
We'll turn that tape over to the transcript typist, and we'll continue to stay up alive through this evasive maneuver.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 10 is now 15,023 miles away from the Earth.
The velocity is 15,364 feet per second.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Me would like you to verify that the suit circuit return value is Open.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Estimate now that we are out a good 1,000 - 800 to 1,000 feet from the S-IVB and it moved out laterally - oh, maybe 400 feet.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: It looks like out of the burn direction that we will be in will put us in the right direction, if we keep moving laterally.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We are in burn attitude at this time.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We'd like for you to turn off the O2CRYO fan.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: MCC is requesting that the crew cease the O2cryogenic stir now that the pressure has returned to desired level.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We were getting a bad squeal on our S-band.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Communications have been switched from the High Gain Antenna to the CM omni antenna, due to interference over the High Gain.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Is that okay, or do you want to look at this one on High Gain.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: It looks good on the omni, if you can stay where we are.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We are going to start our gimbal drive check.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The SPS gimbal motors are powered up at this time to provide SPS control during the S-IVB evasive manoeuvre.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Tom Stafford running some gimbal checks on the SPS engine prior to this evasive maneuver.
We are about 3 minutes, 57 seconds away from that maneuver which will occur at 4 hours, 39 minutes, 9 seconds.
Duration of the burn, 2.8 seconds; delta-V, 19.7 feet per second.
Apollo 10 is 17,529 nautical miles away from the Earth; its velocity, 14,368 feet per second.
The weight of the combined vehicles; 94,271 pounds.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The SPS injector prevalves are two positive solenoid-operated valves, one for each pneumatic control system, and are identified as A and B.
The valve is energized open and spring-loaded closed.
The injector prevalves are controlled by the Delta-V Thrust Normal switches on MDC-1 (Main Display Console 1).
When switch A is placed to Normal, injector prevalve A is energized open.
If switch B is placed to Normal, injector prevalve B is energized open.
The injector prevalves, when energized open, allow GN2supply tank pressure to be directed through an orifice, into a regulator, relief valve, and to a pair of solenoid control valves.
The solenoid control valves are controlled by the SPS thrust On-Off commands.
The Off position of the Delta-V Thrust switches de-energizes the injector prevalves and springloads closed.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Looks like a good burn to us.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We have - You see the residuals, plus one, two-tenths, and five-tenths.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That burn looked real good to us.
I think the SPS is Go.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And we're leaving S-IVB way behind.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: All the postburn items are cleaned up.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Could you give us a - before we pick up the PTC at 12 hours, could you give us an angle so we can look at the Earth out of one of the windows?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We're going to have an update on that Flight Plan maneuver for you.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We're about 95 percent sure that we're going to skip midcourse number 1.
Give us some time to look at the data, and we'll get back and confirm that with you definitely.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: When you doff your suits, we'd like each one of you to read - give us a reading on your suit radiation dosimeters, please.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You want to go ahead and start charging Batt A?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: A constant voltage, solid-state battery charger, located in the CM lower equipment bay, is incorporated into the EPS.
The Battery Charge selector switch [MDC-3] controls power input to the charger, as well as connecting the charger output to the selected battery.
When the Battery Charge selector switch is positioned to entry battery A, B, or C, a relay (Kl) is activated completing circuits from ac and dc power sources to the battery charger.
Battery charger output is also connected to the selected battery to be charged through contacts of the Main Bus Tie motor switch.

Positioning the Main Bus Tie switch (A/C or B/C) [panel 5] to Off for battery A or B, and both switches to Off for battery C will disconnect main bus loads from the respective batteries and also complete the circuit from the charger to the battery.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You can start the Batt A charge.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: When somebody is down in the LEB, we'd like a readout of the LM/CM Delta-P [panel 12].
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The LM/CM Delta-P reading gave the pressure differential between the two modules, so that the crew could confirm the pressure of the LM tunnel prior to opening the CM forward hatch.
A reading of zero psid would indicate that the pressure was the same in the CM and the LM tunnel.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: John's able to get it for you.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: In about 3 minutes, we're going to have a short LOX dump on the Saturn.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: It's just long gone from us.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I'm reading 100.5 percent oxidizer and 101.2 percent fuel.
My unbalance went from on that short burn - from a minus 50 to a minus 200.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: 10, we'll have that attitude for you for the righthand hatch window for the Earth in a couple of seconds.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Is that okay, or do you want the hatch window?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: With the right hand, we could use the mount, then.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The last time I saw it, it started to look - The last time I saw it, it started to look like a medicine ball.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We have an update for your P23 attitudes and stars if you care to copy at this time.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: P23 (program 23) is used for midcourse navigation by taking star/Earth or star/Moon optical measurements, to enable onboard determination of the present state vector.
By combining the angle measured between the planet and the star at a particular point in time, the Apollo Guidance Computer (AGC) can calculate the current state vector, using P23.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: At 5 hours, this attitude for the P23 as listed in the Flight Plan is incorrect.
We'd like you to have a roll of 180, pitch of 167, a yaw of zero.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That's affirmative, and we've got an update to your stars also on page 3-9 [of the Flight Plan] if you're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Set 1 is now Nunki, number 37, and it's near side.
Set 2 is Peacock, number 42, I and it's near horizon.
Set 3 is also Peacock 42, near horizon.
And the last two sets, 4 and 5, are Antares, far horizon.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Did you say Peacock set 3 was far horizon?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Near horizon for both sets 2 and 3 on Peacock.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The reason for the update, we forgot we had Snoop [LM] out there.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We've got that attitude for the Earth out the right-hand window.
It's a roll of 277, pitch 187, yaw 15.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Apollo 10 just passed the 20,000 mile mark.
Now, 20,007 miles from Earth; velocity is 13,508 feet per second, weight 94,096 and one-half pounds.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We are receiving data from Apollo 10.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Having some difficulty with the voice communications.
The communication officer says it looks like Apollo 10 may be trying to lock on with the High Gain Antenna now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I've been having a lot of trouble with the omnis, and the High Gain borrows.
We get about three-fourths signal strength, and the noise gets so bad, and apparently you're not hearing us.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We've been hearing the same thing.
We think you're going from omni to omni a little bit too fast and not giving us a chance to get locked up real good.
Are you with - I guess we're on the omnis now, and we're hearing you five-by.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I'm on omni C right now.
We've got the Earth here, and we'd like to be able to go high gain and show it to you, if you can figure it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We've configured network's ready to go.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: This is Apollo Control; 5 hours, 4 minutes.
Apollo 10 is going to try to bring up another live color TV show, showing the Earth.
We'll stand by for this transmission.
Apollo 10's distance from the Earth now 21,119 nautical miles.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Charlie, if you see this, it's going to be out of this world, literally.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We can see exactly all of...
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Are you getting any signal now?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I'm looking right at the good old U.S.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: How does the High Gain signal strength look to you?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We got you on the black and white.
It looks great on the black and white.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I figure right there you should be able to see the United States, Mexico, Baja California -
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: You ought to see it up here, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We've got the whole globe there.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Yes, you're looking right at the United States there.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: See the Rocky Mountains sticking out?
Can't tell whether you have any smog in LA or not, but Alaska is pretty much socked in.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We'll just let it go here for a couple of minutes.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Hey, Gene-o, on your monitor, which way is the North Pole to you?
We've got it up at the northeast corner?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The northeast corner is the North Pole.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: You can see cloud - covers the northern part of Alaska, and it comes down and cloudcovers over the northeastern part of Canada, and I can see out into the New England area.
We've got a low pressure area out there.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: And it looks like the Rocky Mountains are orange colored to me.
The rest of U.S., Baja California, that really stands out as all brownish, and the oceans are blue; but there are so many clouds out to the northeast of the United States, you can't believe it.
Covers the Far East over to Europe as far as you can see.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We've got a brownish spot that's pretty hard to pick out just exactly what we're looking at, but we do see the brown and the clouds out over the ocean about the center of the globe.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The brown spot is the Rocky Mountains.
It runs down around into New Mexico, up into Colorado.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The LM, you can count the rivets on it, and yet the Earth and the colors are really beautiful.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Well, I can zoom in a bit on a certain part of the Earth here; I'll try to zoom in on the Rocky Mountains and California.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We're looking at it on the black and white.
Here he comes in on the color now.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We can pick out Baja and the smog over LA and we see Mexico and we go off to the east, in our picture, and come into the Rockies area.
The Baja California and the Gulf, they really stand out beautiful.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Looks like we've got some cloud down over New Orleans and down in that way.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, this has got to be the greatest sight ever.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You ought to see it up here.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Could you get Baja in a little bit?
We're showing this deal to the Pacific now.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We've got the Baja now on the right-hand side of our screen, and it's right in the center now, and we can see Mexico and the clouds up over the Rockies.
It's really a fantastic sight, almost see the freeways in LA.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, I'm going to move down.
You can take a look down by the Gulf of Mexico now.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Charlie, this is - It's so hard to describe.
You can go right up past Alaska, and you can see the polar caps.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We see it all here, Gene.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And the blackest black that you ever could conceive is the setting for all this.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Looks like the people of New York have a little bit of cloud cover today.
It goes all the way down, in fact, down back by the Cape.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: You guys are really giving us a great show.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We just want to thank all the peop1e who helped get us up here, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Charlie, you know, you blink your eyes and you look out there and you know it's three dimensional, but it is just sitting out there in the middle of nowhere and it's unbelievable.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We are getting a real idea now, for the first time, of what you are really seeing up there.
The colors on the oceans are just as blue as they can be and real white clouds all over, and the land is a real deep brown, Almost a reddish brown.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And the North Pole, the Arctic area, is just solidly, whitely, snow-covered.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It's a brown around in the Rocky Mountains and orange down into New Mexico and becomes a little more of a purplish orange...
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We see all - I cut you off there, Tom.
We see all that, and it is looking good.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You can actually pick out what I think is the San Joaquin Valley down there, just on the west side of the hills.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, I am going to open the zoom and bring it back in the other way.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Would you check your TV servo power.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I must have knocked it on there when I was wrestling around here.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Boy, we are looking at a black background now.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: There, you have the whole Earth.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Tom, we can even pick up the little tip of the northern part of South America down below Baja.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We are looking right at you.
Looks like you may have a few clouds there right now in Houston.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It's sort of a constant overcast here in the MOCR but we have never been...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You are right on the edge of what might be some clouds, but certainly to the northeast of that area, it's clobbered.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: John is going ahead through P52 [IMU realignment] here.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I think I can see all the way up into the Newfoundland area now, up along the eastern coast that is not covered.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It looks to us that it is just completely clouded up over that way.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You might say we have moved out in the last couple of hours.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We got you at about 26,000 miles here - or a little bit more.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I am going to put the zoom on 55, which will give you the exact resolution we have.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: And, just for the record, it looks to me like a pretty nice place to live.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And we see you at P52.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: 55 on the zoom, and that is about exactly the same thing we are seeing now.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: 10, can you see the Hawaiian Islands?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Those Gyro torquing angles look okay to you?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: They look okay to us, John.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You can't verify these stars with this LM on right now.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: How do the stars look in this attitude?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Houston, in the telescope, I have difficulty telling the stars from the particles, right now.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: There are some that look like stars but I have not been able to stop long enough to really see if they are or not.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Through the sextant they are okay?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The Earth is just starting now to take on - a little bit of the terminator is coming across and we are losing the roundness on the bottom side.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I should say on the eastern side.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We are seeing that now - coming in.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I hope the colors are as good down there as they are up here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That is the only way I can describe it.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Does that picture fill up your whole screen now?
I want to correlate it with the monitor.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We got about - quite a ways to go before we fill up our screen, Tom.
It looks about the size of a basketball, sort of.
It's pretty hard, you are zooming in now and we are just about to fill up the whole picture.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The monitor has a little different aspect ratio than the standard view.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We are ready for the P27 [AGC update] update, if you can give us P00 and Accept.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We want to get busy here.
We are going to kill the TV for a while.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Thanks a lot for a good show, 10.
Right about now, from top to bottom, we have the whole Earth, and we still have some on the side.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We'll go off the air now, Charlie, for a while.
But, doggone, I'm glad that came through to you.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Thanks a lot again for the show.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Guess we know somebody that's crying in his beer.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Yes - No, I think he is happy, too.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We'd like for you to put the S-band squelch switch Off [panel 180 LEB] and when you're changing omnis, to pause 30 seconds in each position so we can get a good lockup.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The S-band Squelch circuit removes unwanted noise from the S-band up-link signal.
This switch and circuit was only fitted on CM-106 (Apollo 10) & CM-107 (Apollo 11).
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 5 hours, 21 minutes.
Tom Stafford has indicated they are going to close up shop for a little while as far as communications are concerned.
Apollo 10 is 23,059 miles away from the Earth.
We'll come back up if there is any more air to ground.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: How are you reading our omni?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: 10, this is Houston, And we're reading you loud and clear.
We had a little noise there a minute or so ago.
It sounded like the S-band was getting weak.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We're in now and I believe we're on the omni D right now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You can have the computer back and Up Telemetry to Block and on your primary evaporator we'd like you to secure the H2O flow.
That is H2O flow to Off.
We recommend not attempting to service the system until after LOI.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: CapCom Duke is referring back to the problem encountered during launch with the primary evaporator drying out.
The evaporator system will be reserviced with water just prior to LOI.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Just for information, I don't know how far away, it's far away, but we can tell that there is still a SLA panel out there just spinning around slowly and reflecting sunlight.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Do you have any more information on apparent size, range, or anything like this on it?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: No, it's the size of Venus but it's obviously a SLA panel because you can see it rotating slowly in reflected light.
transcript.speaker: Cernan
